leftRecord.heading: Tchaikovsky, Peter Ilich, 1840-1893
knownFor: Piano Concerto No. 1 in B-flat minor

evidence: Identical birth and death years (1840, 1893) and identical nationality and field across both records.
Both records resolve to the same Russian-Cyrillic original name form: Пётр Ильич Чайковский.
LC uses ALA-LC romanization; DNB uses the ISO 9 scientific transliteration. The strings differ; the person does not.
Each record carries the other as a 4XX cross-reference (see-from): LC has 'Čajkovskij' as a non-preferred form; DNB has 'Tchaikovsky' as a non-preferred form.
VIAF cluster 40177743 unifies twenty-plus national-authority records, each using its own jurisdiction's transliteration convention while pointing at one identity.

explanation: Transliteration is the quietest hard problem in authority work. The two strings above look like different people to anyone who reads only one alphabet — and the visitor's intuition that they might be different is exactly what makes this case pedagogically useful. The discipline has handled the multi-alphabet world by treating each transliteration as a non-preferred name form pointing at one canonical identity, usually in the source script (Cyrillic here). The catalog does not pretend a single Latin-script spelling is correct; it keeps all of them, each labeled with its rules of origin, and lets the cluster carry the consensus. Modern entity-resolution systems trained on Latin-only corpora still routinely fail this case — the discipline solved it in the mid-twentieth century, by acknowledging that the canonical form was the one no transliteration system would ever fully capture.
